Here is the final answer from your managed agent 'search_agent':
### 1. Task outcome (short version):
The search yielded relevant studies by Manash Pratim Kashyap and PS Fader on customer retention published in 2018 and 2019, specifically involving methodologies to analyze customer retention such as beta-geometric and the beta-discrete-Weibull models.

### 2. Task outcome (extremely detailed version):
Manash Pratim Kashyap's notable study titled "A Simple Model for Analyzing the Customer Retention Comparing Rural and Urban Store" was published in March 2019. This paper analyzes customer retention rates in rural versus urban settings, though specific two-word model types were not explicitly outlined in the findings reported in search extracts.

For PS Fader, several significant papers were identified:
- **"In Pursuit of Enhanced Customer Retention Management"** (2018) discusses various management strategies and metrics to enhance customer retention, though it primarily focuses on broader retention strategies without specific model names.
- **"How to Project Customer Retention Revisited"** (2018) introduces the beta-geometric (BG) distribution for projecting customer retention. It also presents the beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution as an extension of the BG model, allowing for temporal changes in churn probabilities. This is particularly important as it challenges conventional assumptions about customer behavior longevity.

Thus, the two-word model types discussed in Fader's works are: **beta-geometric (BG) distribution** and **beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution**. These models are critical for understanding customer retention dynamics, suggesting that assumptions about constancy in churn rates may not hold true over time.

### 3. Additional context (if relevant):
Both authors contribute significantly to the field of customer retention, with Fader's work focusing on statistical modeling of retention rates. In contrast, Kashyap's focus appears to be more aligned with practical market analysis. The complexities in these studies highlight the evolving understanding of customer retention in response to diverse consumer behaviors.

For more detail, find below a summary of this agent's work:

[{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Observation:\nAddress: file:///home/jerry/gaia_agents/smolagents/examples/open_deep_research/downloads_folder/ascarza_et_al_cns_17_e08d63cf-0b65-4526-9d23-b0b09dcee9b9_538a6ea6-a480-4841-b9f0-a87be24989ba__13.pdf\nViewport position: Showing page 1 of 17.\n=======================\nCust. Need. and Solut.\nhttps://doi.org/10.1007/s40547-017-0080-0\n\nRESEARCH ARTICLE\n\nIn Pursuit of Enhanced Customer Retention Management:\nReview, Key Issues, and Future Directions\n\nEva Ascarza 1 & Scott A. Neslin 2 & Oded Netzer 1\n& Zachery Anderson 3 & Peter S. Fader 4 &\nSunil Gupta 5 & Bruce G. S. Hardie 6 & Aurélie Lemmens 7 & Barak Libai 8 & David Neal 9 &\nFoster Provost 10 & Rom Schrift 4\n\n# Springer Science+Business Media, LLC 2017\n\nAbstract In today’s turbulent business environment, custom-\ner retention presents a significant challenge for many service\ncompanies. Academics have generated a large body of re-\nsearch that addresses part of that challenge—with a particular\nfocus on predicting customer churn. However, several other\nequally important aspects of managing retention have not re-\nceived similar level of attention, leaving many managerial\nproblems not completely solved, and a program of academic\nresearch not completely aligned with managerial needs.\nTherefore, our goal is to draw on previous research and\n\ncurrent practice to provide insights on managing retention\nand identify areas for future research. This examination leads\nus to advocate a broad perspective on customer retention. We\npropose a definition that extends the concept beyond the tra-\nditional binary retain/not retain view of retention. We discuss a\nvariety of metrics to measure and monitor retention. We pres-\nent an integrated framework for managing retention that le-\nverages emerging opportunities offered by new data sources\nand new methodologies such as machine learning. We high-\nlight the importance of distinguishing between which\n\nThis paper is the outcome of a workshop on BCustomer Retention^ as part\nof the 10th Triennial Invitational Choice Symposium, University of\nAlberta, 2016.\n\n* Eva Ascarza\n\nAscarza@columbia.edu\n\nScott A. Neslin\nscott.a.neslin@dartmouth.edu\n\nOded Netzer\nonetzer@gsb.columbia.edu\n\nZachery Anderson\nzanderson@ea.com\n\nPeter S. Fader\nfaderp@wharton.upenn.edu\n\nSunil Gupta\nsgupta@hbs.edu\n\nBruce G. S. Hardie\nbhardie@london.edu\n\nAurélie Lemmens\na.lemmens@tilburguniversity.edu\n\nBarak Libai\nlibai@idc.ac.il\n\nDavid Neal\ndavid@catalystbehavioral.com\n\nFoster Provost\nfprovost@stern.nyu.edu\n\nRom Schrift\nroms@wharton.upenn.edu\n\n1 Columbia Business School, New York, NY, USA\n2\n\nTuck School of Business, Hanover, NH, USA\n\n3\n\nElectronic Arts, Redwood City, CA, USA\n\n4\n\nThe Wharton School, Philadelphia, PA, USA\n5 Harvard Business School, Boston, MA, USA\n6\n\nLondon Business School, London, UK\n\n7\n\nTilburg School of Economics and Management, Tilburg, Netherlands\n\n8 Arison School of Business, Hertsliya, Israel\n9 Catalyst Behavioral Sciences and Duke University, Durham, NC,\n\nUSA\n\n10 New York University, New York, NY, USA\n\n\x0ccustomers are at risk and which should be targeted—as they\nare not necessarily the same customers. We identify trade-offs\nbetween reactive and proactive retention programs, between\nshort- and long-term remedies, and between discrete cam-\npaigns and continuous processes for managing retention. We\nidentify several areas of research where further investigation\nwill significantly enhance retention management.\n\nWe therefore see managers struggling with how to manage\nretention appropriately and an academic community not fo-\ncused on all the important issues. Therein motivates the pur-\npose of this paper, to draw on previous research and current\npractice to distill insights and key issues regarding customer\nretention and to propose avenues for future research. In par-\nticular, we propose:\n\nCust. Need. and Solut.\n\nKeywords Customer retention . Churn . Customer\nrelationship management (CRM)\n\n1 In Pursuit of Enhanced Customer Retention\nManagement\n\nThe central purpose of managing customer relationships\nis for the enterprise to focus on increasing the overall\nvalue of its customer base—and customer retention is\ncritical to its success.\n([66], p. 15)\n\nCustomer relationship management (CRM) managers and\nacademics have long recognized the centrality and, in fact, the\nimperative of retaining customers. Customer retention is a\ncornerstone of broader CRM concepts such as customer equi-\nty [14, 76] and is arguably the most important component of\nthe customer lifetime value (CLV) framework [37].\n\nYet, there are indications that companies have problems\nmanaging customer retention. From the customer point of\nview, 85% of customers report that companies could do more\nto retain them [40]. From the firm’s point of view, while the\nvast majority of top executives report that customer retention\nis a priority within their organization, 49% of them admit to\nbeing unhappy with their ability to support their retention\ngoals [33]. Furthermore, there is growing evidence that reten-\ntion campaigns can be futile or even harmful [2, 4, 12].\n\nRetention is especially important in the digital environ-\nment. For example, MusicWatch reported that only 48% of\nthose who tried Apple Music, a music streaming service, still\nwere using the service 2 months after it was launched [82,\n100]. Similarly, for mobile apps, it is reported that, across all\ncategories, 75% of all app users churn\nAddress: file:///home/jerry/gaia_agents/smolagents/examples/open_deep_research/downloads_folder/BdW_JIM_2018-01-10__21.pdf\nViewport position: Showing page 1 of 19.\n=======================\n“How to Project Customer Retention” Revisited:\nThe Role of Duration Dependence\n\nPeter S. Fader\nBruce G. S. Hardie\nYuzhou Liu\nJoseph Davin\nThomas Steenburgh†\n\nJanuary 2018\n\n†Peter S. Fader is the Frances and Pei-Yuan Chia Professor of Marketing at The Wharton School of\nthe University of Pennsylvania (address: 771 Jon M. Huntsman Hall, 3730 Walnut Street, Philadelphia,\nPA 19104-6340; phone: (215) 898-1132; email: faderp@wharton.upenn.edu; web: www.petefader.com).\nBruce G. S. Hardie is Professor of Marketing at the London Business School (email: bhardie@london.edu;\nweb: www.brucehardie.com). Yuzhou Liu is a Quantitative Researcher at Man Numeric. Joseph Davin is\nHead of Data Science, Two Six Capital. Thomas Steenburgh is the Bank of America Research Professor\nof Business Administration at the Darden Graduate School of Business, University of Virginia.\n\n\x0cAbstract\n\n“How to Project Customer Retention” Revisited:\nThe Role of Duration Dependence\n\nCohort-level retention rates typically increase over time, and the beta-geometric (BG) distribu-\ntion has proven to be a robust model for capturing and projecting these patterns into the future.\nAccording to this model, the phenomenon of increasing cohort-level retention rates is purely due\nto cross-sectional heterogeneity; an individual customer’s propensity to churn does not change\nover time. In this paper we present the beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution as an exten-\nsion to the BG model, one that allows individual-level churn probabilities to increase or decrease\nover time. In addition to capturing the phenomenon of increasing cohort-level retention rates,\nthis new model can also accommodate situations in which there is an initial dip in retention\nrates before they increase (i.e., a U-shaped cohort-level retention curve). A key ﬁnding is that\neven when aggregate retention rates are monotonically increasing, the individual-level churn\nprobabilities are unlikely to be declining over time, as conventional wisdom would suggest. We\ncarefully explore these connections between heterogeneity, duration dependence, and the shape\nof the retention curve, and draw some managerially relevant conclusions, e.g., that account-\ning for cross-sectional heterogeneity is more important than accounting for any individual-level\ndynamics in churn propensities.\n\nKeywords: beta-geometric (BG) distribution, beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribu-\n\ntion, retention rate dynamics.\n\n\x0c1\n\nIntroduction\n\nAny researcher working with data from a business that has a “contractual” relationship with its\n\ncustomers (e.g., one with a subscription-based business model) will want models for projecting\n\ncustomer retention (or equivalently, tenure) as part of their toolkit. For example, estimates of\n\nthe length of a customer’s relationship with the ﬁrm lie at the heart of any attempt to compute\n\ncustomer lifetime value (CLV). Similarly, such models are useful when evaluating the relative\n\nperformance of diﬀerent acquisition channels.\n\nFader and Hardie (2007), hereafter FH, presented the beta-geometric (BG) distribution as a\n\nsimple probability model for projecting customer retention. This model is based on an easy-to-\n\nunderstand “story” of customer behavior, is simple to implement (e.g., can be done so in Excel),\n\nand its estimates of customer retention over a longitudinal holdout period have proven to be\n\nsurprisingly accurate and robust.\n\nAccording to this model, the widely observed phenomenon of increasing cohort-level retention\n\nrates (Reichheld 1996) is purely due to cross-sectional heterogeneity, with individual customers\n\nhaving a constant propensity to churn. Cohort-level retention rates increase because those\n\ncustomers with high churn propensities drop out early on, leaving an ever-increasing proportion\n\nof customers who have low propensities to churn. This assumption that an individual customer’s\n\npropensity to churn does not change over time ﬂies in the face of conventional wisdom, which\n\nassumes that a customer’s propensity to churn decreases the longer their tenure with the ﬁrm.\n\nWhile cohort-level retention rates are typically monotonically increasing with tenure, we\n\nsometimes observe an initial dip before they increase (e.g., Israel 2005, Nitzan et al. 2011, and\n\none of the FH datasets), a phenomenon that the BG model cannot capture. In this paper we\n\ndevelop a generalization of the BG model, one that both relaxes the assumption of time-invariant\n\nindividual-level propensities to churn and is suﬃciently ﬂexible to capture the phenomenon of\n\nnon-monotonically increasing cohort-level retention rates. Surprisingly, we ﬁnd that when the\n\nassumption of constant individual-level propensities to churn is violated, it is more likely that\n\nthese propensities increase with tenure (rather than decrease, as conventional wisdom would\n\nsuggest).\n\nThis paper is organized as follows.\n\nIn the next section we re-examine the work of FH,\n\n1\n\n\x0creviewing the BG model and its empirical performance. We then present our generalization\n\nof the BG model, the beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution, and examine its performance\n\nusing the'}]

Task outcome (short version):\nThe search yielded relevant studies by Manash Pratim Kashyap and PS Fader on customer retention published in 2018 and 2019, specifically involving methodologies to analyze customer retention such as beta-geometric and the beta-discrete-Weibull models.\n\n### 2. Task outcome (extremely detailed version):\nManash Pratim Kashyap\'s notable study titled "A Simple Model for Analyzing the Customer Retention Comparing Rural and Urban Store" was published in March 2019. This paper analyzes customer retention rates in rural versus urban settings, though specific two-word model types were not explicitly outlined in the findings reported in search extracts.\n\nFor PS Fader, several significant papers were identified:\n- **"In Pursuit of Enhanced Customer Retention Management"** (2018) discusses various management strategies and metrics to enhance customer retention, though it primarily focuses on broader retention strategies without specific model names.\n- **"How to Project Customer Retention Revisited"** (2018) introduces the beta-geometric (BG) distribution for projecting customer retention. It also presents the beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution as an extension of the BG model, allowing for temporal changes in churn probabilities. This is particularly important as it challenges conventional assumptions about customer behavior longevity.\n\nThus, the two-word model types discussed in Fader\'s works are: **beta-geometric (BG) distribution** and **beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution**. These models are critical for understanding customer retention dynamics, suggesting that assumptions about constancy in churn rates may not hold true over time.\n\n### 3. Additional context (if relevant):\nBoth authors contribute significantly to the field of customer retention, with Fader\'s work focusing on statistical modeling of retention rates. In contrast, Kashyap\'s focus appears to be more aligned with practical market analysis. The complexities in these studies highlight the evolving understanding of customer retention in response to diverse consumer behaviors.'}]
